texto: ...I was there when we opened the gates. Some of these poor wretches running out were so emaciated they actually died from the excitement of being liberated. I saw it happen several times. These people in the camps – they were like walking skeletons. You could see all their bones. The gates opened and the people ran out yelling, "I'm free! I'm free!" And some of them died right there. I was horrified to see what the SS had done to these people. - Roy Gates
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Marcus Brotherton: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “...I was there when we opened the gates. Some of these poor wretches running out were so emaciate…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que tempo, concentration-camps, holocaust pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de holocaust e sad.

frase_original: ...I was there when we opened the gates. Some of these poor wretches running out were so emaciated they actually died from the excitement of being liberated. I saw it happen several times. These people in the camps – they were like walking skeletons. You could see all their bones. The gates opened and the people ran out yelling, "I'm free! I'm free!" And some of them died right there. I was horrified to see what the SS had done to these people. - Roy Gates

texto: Our heroes are over there where the white crosses are. We're survivors over here. None of us are heroes. I don't think you'll talk to a man who say we are. You figure a hero is someone who does above and beyond the call of duty, and when you give your life that's as above and beyond as you can get- Earl McClung.
explicacao: Citação de Marcus Brotherton sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “Our heroes are over there where the white crosses are. We're survivors over here. None of us are …” articula reflexao, duty, heroes em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como heroes.
